observation_summary: The JsonParserSequence skips a token when switching between concatenated parsers, causing the sequence to return the wrong token value (3 instead of 2).
hypothesis: The nextToken() implementation in JsonParserSequence incorrectly calls nextToken() on the new delegate parser immediately upon switching, which consumes the first token of the second parser prematurely.
prediction: The code in JsonParserSequence.nextToken() will show an unconditional call to nextToken() on the delegate parser when switching, without checking if the delegate is already positioned at a valid token.
experiment_rationale: The bug report explicitly states that the nextToken() method calls nextToken() on the new delegate, causing a token to be missed. This is a procedural logic error in how the sequence manages the transition between parsers.
reasoning_summary: The defect is a procedural error in the sequence's iteration logic (Algorithm/Method). It is not a missing guard (Checking), a wrong constant (Assignment/Initialization), or a design-level capability issue (Function/Class/Object). The logic for advancing the parser sequence is flawed because it forces an extra advancement step during the transition.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: While a check might be added to prevent the skip, the root cause is the procedural logic of the sequence advancement itself, which is an algorithmic flaw in the state transition.
The issue is not a mismatch in the interface contract between the sequence and the parsers, but rather the internal logic of the sequence class itself.
